skill.name: github-actions-templates
skill.description: Create GitHub Actions workflow templates for testing, Docker builds, Kubernetes deployments, matrix builds, reusable workflows, security scans, and approval-gated releases.
skill.summary: Generate practical GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow patterns for common build, test, scan, and deployment needs.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many command, credential, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Most are false positives caused by Markdown GitHub Actions examples, but the templates include mutable third-party action references and long-lived AWS credential examples that need publication warnings.

medium_findings.title: Mutable Third-Party Action References
medium_findings.description: The security scan examples use actions referenced with the mutable master branch. This is a supply-chain risk because future upstream changes would alter workflow behavior without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference third-party actions with @master. Mutable action refs are a well-known CI supply-chain risk, even though the examples are not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Long-Lived Cloud Credential Template
medium_findings.description: The Kubernetes deployment example configures AWS access key and secret access key values from GitHub secrets. This is a legitimate pattern, but it encourages long-lived cloud credentials instead of short-lived OIDC federation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly uses AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY from secrets. This is not exfiltration, but it creates avoidable credential exposure if copied directly.

medium_findings.title: Privileged CI Command Templates
medium_findings.description: The templates include shell commands that update kubeconfig, apply Kubernetes manifests, inspect production resources, install dependencies, run tests, and deploy applications. These are expected for CI/CD, but copied workflows can execute powerful operations in user repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in workflow examples and are appropriate for the skill purpose. Risk depends on repository permissions, runner trust, and user customization.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown YAML snippets and reference text, not code that the skill executes. They should be treated as workflow content that users may copy, not runtime behavior of the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is SKILL.md and the relevant sections are fenced YAML examples or reference bullets. No executable script file or hidden runtime launcher is present in the reported file structure.

low_findings.title: Credential References Use GitHub Secrets Context
low_findings.description: The token and secret references use GitHub Actions secrets syntax. This is normal for workflow templates and does not show hardcoded credentials or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders under the GitHub secrets context. No literal secret value or external transfer of those secrets was found in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Placeholder Metadata
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is an example production environment URL in a deployment template. It does not receive data or trigger a network request by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is https://app.example.com inside an environment metadata field. It is not used as an endpoint for sending credentials or repository data.

low_findings.title: Hidden Path Is Standard Workflow Location
low_findings.description: The hidden file access finding points to .github/workflows in a reusable workflow example. This is the standard GitHub Actions workflow directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is used in a workflow_call example for a local reusable workflow. It is expected for GitHub Actions and is not evidence of stealthy filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Lack Source Evidence
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations do not contain cryptographic operations in the reviewed source context. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines showed skill metadata, a kubectl describe command, and a related-skill reference. These do not implement cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable CI Action Version Pins
dangerous_patterns.description: Third-party actions are pinned to master, which can change without repository review. Use immutable commit SHAs or trusted version tags before publishing production templates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both cited lines contain @master action references. The risk is supply-chain drift rather than confirmed malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Deployment Secrets In CI
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment example passes AWS credentials into a CI job. Users should prefer OIDC, least privilege permissions, and protected environments for production deployments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited block configures AWS credentials from GitHub secrets. This is common but has clear operational security impact.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time rebuilding CI/CD workflows for each repository. This skill provides practical GitHub Actions templates for testing, packaging, scanning, and deployment.
actual_capabilities: Drafts test workflows for Node.js and Python projects.
Creates Docker build and container registry publishing workflows.
Provides Kubernetes deployment workflow patterns with rollout checks.
Shows matrix builds across operating systems and language versions.
Includes reusable workflow and security scanning examples.
limitations: Templates need review before use in production repositories.
Cloud deployment examples require project-specific permissions and secrets.
It does not verify third-party action versions or vendor trust.
It does not replace security review of generated CI commands.

use_cases.title: Set Up Repository CI
use_cases.description: Create a test workflow that installs dependencies, runs linters, runs tests, and uploads coverage for pull requests.

use_cases.title: Publish Container Images
use_cases.description: Build and push Docker images to a registry with metadata, tags, permissions, and cache settings.

use_cases.title: Standardize Release Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design reusable workflows for deployment, security scanning, approval gates, and environment-specific release steps.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Test Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for pull request checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow for a Node.js project. It should run on pull requests, install dependencies, run linting, run tests, and upload coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Docker Build Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that builds a Docker image, tags it from branches and releases, caches layers, and pushes it to GitHub Container Registry.

prompt_templates.title: Matrix CI Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a matrix workflow for a Python project. Test multiple Python versions on Linux, macOS, and Windows, and keep the workflow readable.

prompt_templates.title: Production Deployment Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production deployment workflow with security scanning, environment approvals, least privilege permissions, reusable workflow structure, and safer cloud authentication guidance.

output_examples.input: Request: Add pull request checks for a Node.js repository.
output_examples.output: A workflow plan with checkout, Node setup, dependency caching, install, lint, test, and coverage upload steps.

output_examples.input: Request: Publish a Docker image when a release tag is pushed.
output_examples.output: A container workflow outline with registry login, metadata generation, build cache, image tags, and package write permissions.

output_examples.input: Request: Deploy to Kubernetes after production approval.
output_examples.output: A deployment workflow outline with protected environment approval, cloud authentication notes, kubeconfig update, manifest apply, and rollout verification.

best_practices: Pin third-party actions to trusted versions or immutable commit SHAs.
Use least privilege permissions and protected environments for deployment jobs.
Replace sample secrets, regions, clusters, and namespaces with reviewed project values.
anti_patterns: Do not copy deployment commands into production without review.
Do not use long-lived cloud keys when OIDC federation is available.
Do not reference mutable action branches for sensitive workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates GitHub Actions workflow templates for testing, builds, Docker publishing, Kubernetes deployment, security scanning, and approvals.

faq.question: Can I use the templates directly?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points. Review permissions, secrets, action versions, commands, and environment names before running them.

faq.question: Does it support reusable workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow_call pattern that can centralize common testing or deployment steps.

faq.question: Does it handle cloud credentials securely?
faq.answer: It shows GitHub secrets examples. For production, prefer OIDC, short-lived credentials, and least privilege access.

faq.question: Which project types are covered?
faq.answer: The examples cover Node.js, Python, Docker containers, Kubernetes deployments, and general security scanning workflows.

faq.question: Does it audit my existing workflow files?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates and guidance, but it does not perform a full security audit of an existing repository.
